personality_voice.book_baseline: Fred is defined by irreverent humor, mischief, and a sharp wit deployed with confidence and timing. He teases Percy relentlessly, mocks authority without fear, uses deadpan delivery and comic escalation (the 'Once / Twice / A minute / All summer' exchange about Percy's prefect badge), and shows genuine warmth beneath the jokes. He is bold, entrepreneurial, and emotionally present in crisis — he falls silent and goes pale when George is injured, and his grief at Fred's own death scene is foreshadowed by his rare moments of stillness. His voice is characterized by short, punchy lines, mock-formality, and a consistent refusal to be cowed. He also shows real courage and moral clarity: confronting Sirius about risking their father's life, defending Harry against Umbridge, and choosing to leave Hogwarts on his own terms.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films preserve Fred's core comedic identity well. The King's Cross twin-swap joke (Film Scene 1), the Marauder's Map handover with theatrical flair (Film Scenes 9-10), the Aging Potion attempt with gleeful confidence (Film Scene 13), the Polyjuice banter ('imagine if something went wrong and we were stuck as scrawny, specky gits forever' — Film Scene 21), and the George ear-wound exchange ('With the whole wide world of ear-related humor at your disposal, you go for holey? Pathetic.' — Film Scene 22) all faithfully reproduce his voice. His short, punchy delivery and mock-solemnity are intact. The 'Nice night for it!' line during the Battle of Hogwarts (Film Scene 24) is a direct book echo.
personality_voice.difference: The films compress Fred and George into a near-indistinguishable unit more than the books do. In the books, Fred has slightly more individual voice — he is more often the initiator, the one who speaks first, the one who escalates. The films distribute lines between them more interchangeably. Several of Fred's most distinctive solo moments are absent: his deadpan Percy-badge escalation, his serious confrontation with Sirius at Grimmauld Place ('Easy for you to say, stuck here!'), and his emotional weight during the Weasley family's worst moments (waiting for news of Arthur, the long night at Grimmauld Place). His mock-formality and solo wit are slightly flattened into a double-act.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality — irreverent, bold, warm beneath the jokes, entrepreneurially driven — is preserved with strong fidelity. The compression into a twin unit and loss of some solo voice moments warrants a minor penalty. The most important traits, flaws, and tone are intact.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Fred serves multiple narrative functions: comic relief, moral counterweight to authority (Percy, Umbridge), plot enabler (Marauder's Map, Extendable Ears, Skiving Snackboxes, Potterwatch), emotional anchor for the Weasley family, and active agent in the resistance against Umbridge. He initiates the Marauder's Map gift, organizes the Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes business, leads the fireworks rebellion, confronts Sirius directly about the Order's secrecy, and volunteers for the Seven Potters mission. He is not passive — he drives scenes and changes outcomes.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The films preserve his most iconic agency moments: the Marauder's Map handover (Film Scenes 9-10), the Aging Potion attempt (Film Scene 13), the fireworks rebellion (Film Scene 19), the Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes tour (Film Scene 20), and the Seven Potters transformation (Film Scene 21). His 'Give her hell from us, Peeves' departure is retained in spirit through the fireworks scene. He volunteers for the Seven Potters mission and participates in the Battle of Hogwarts.
narrative_role_agency.difference: Several significant agency moments are absent or reduced. His confrontation with Sirius at Grimmauld Place ('Easy for you to say, stuck here! I don't see you risking your neck!') — a moment where Fred challenges an adult Order member directly and articulates the family's fear — is cut. His role in Potterwatch (broadcasting as 'Rapier') is present in the book corpus but absent from the film corpus provided. His active role in organizing the DA meeting logistics (buying Butterbeers, signing the parchment first) is reduced. The Extendable Ears subplot, which gives Fred and George genuine intelligence-gathering agency, is compressed. Some of his plot-enabling functions (Skiving Snackboxes enabling Umbridge's classroom chaos) are shown but his authorship of the chaos is less foregrounded.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The core agency is preserved — he initiates, enables, and acts. But the loss of the Sirius confrontation removes a moment where Fred exercises moral agency against an authority figure on behalf of his family, and the Potterwatch role (a significant narrative function in book 7) is absent from the film corpus. These are meaningful reductions.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Fred's motivations are layered: genuine love of mischief and humor as a worldview, entrepreneurial ambition (the joke shop is a serious life goal, not just a hobby), loyalty to family and friends, and a principled resistance to authority he considers unjust or hypocritical. His internal conflict is subtle — he genuinely worries about his mother's approval, feels the weight of the family's financial precarity, and in crisis moments (Arthur's injury, George's wounding) drops the humor entirely, revealing deep emotional investment beneath the performance.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The films show his entrepreneurial drive clearly (Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes, Film Scene 20), his loyalty (Seven Potters, Battle of Hogwarts), and his humor as a genuine worldview rather than a mask. The George ear-wound scene (Film Scene 22) shows the emotional depth beneath the jokes. His resistance to Umbridge is shown as principled, not merely mischievous.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The films do not show the financial motivation behind the joke shop as clearly — in the books, Fred and George's awareness of the family's poverty and their desire to provide for themselves is explicit. The conflict with Mrs. Weasley over their career choice (she wants them in the Ministry) is present in the book corpus but largely absent from the films. The scene where Fred defends giving Dudley the Ton-Tongue Toffee ('We gave it to him because he's a great bullying git') — showing his moral reasoning — is absent from the film corpus.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified but not wrong. The entrepreneurial drive and loyalty are intact. The financial dimension and the family-career conflict are underexplained but not contradicted. Minor simplification warrants a modest penalty.

character_arc.book_baseline: Fred's arc moves from mischievous schoolboy to serious entrepreneur and resistance fighter. Key stages: establishing the joke shop ambition (books 4-5), leaving Hogwarts on his own terms as an act of principled defiance (book 5), opening Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes (book 6), joining the Order's resistance and Potterwatch (book 7), and dying in the Battle of Hogwarts mid-laugh — his death is the culmination of a life lived defiantly joyful. The arc is not one of internal transformation but of escalating commitment to his values.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The films track the major arc beats: the joke shop ambition is established, the Hogwarts departure is shown (Film Scene 19), the shop is seen in operation (Film Scene 20), he participates in the Seven Potters mission and the Battle of Hogwarts (Film Scenes 21, 24). His death occurs off-screen between Film Scenes 23 and the implied aftermath, consistent with the book.
character_arc.difference: The Potterwatch arc (Fred as 'Rapier,' broadcasting resistance radio) is absent from the film corpus, removing a significant step in his transition from schoolboy prankster to active resistance fighter. The book's careful escalation — from school mischief to organized resistance — is compressed. The emotional weight of his departure from Hogwarts is present but the buildup (the swamp, the fireworks as deliberate acts of war against Umbridge) is somewhat reduced in the films.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc's major beats are present and the trajectory is faithful. The missing Potterwatch step and some compression of the resistance escalation warrant a moderate penalty — the arc is compressed but still recognizable.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Fred's most important relationships: (1) George — a genuine partnership of equals, not just comic doubling; they have private conversations, separate concerns, and individual moments of vulnerability. (2) Mrs. Weasley — a loving but genuinely conflicted relationship; she disapproves of their career, they defy her, but the Grimmauld Place reunion after the World Cup attack shows real mutual love. (3) Harry — Fred treats Harry as a peer and genuine friend, gives him the Marauder's Map, accepts his Triwizard winnings, and defends him. (4) Percy — Fred's contempt for Percy's pomposity is a running thread that becomes genuinely painful when Percy estranges himself from the family.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Fred-George dynamic is preserved as a partnership, though the films lean more heavily into their interchangeability. The Fred-Harry relationship is shown through the Map handover and the Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes scene. The Fred-Mrs. Weasley dynamic is present (the Burrow arrival scene, Film Scene 4; the Grimmauld Place scenes). The Percy reconciliation in the Battle of Hogwarts is present in the book corpus and implied in the film.
key_relationships.difference: The Fred-George relationship loses some individual texture — in the books, they have private conversations and separate concerns (the Bagman blackmail subplot, their individual reactions to crises). The Fred-Percy dynamic, which is a sustained thread across multiple books, is compressed in the films. The emotional complexity of Fred's relationship with his mother — genuine love coexisting with real defiance — is present but less developed. The Bagman subplot (Fred and George's attempt to recover their World Cup winnings) is entirely absent from the films, removing a relationship that shows Fred's persistence and moral indignation.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The core dynamics are recognizable but the Fred-George individual texture and the Fred-Percy sustained dynamic are reduced. The Bagman subplot's absence removes a relationship that reveals Fred's character. Moderate penalty.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Fred's complexity lies in the gap between his comic persona and his emotional depth. He is genuinely funny but also genuinely brave, genuinely loving, and capable of real anger and grief. Key nuance moments: his silence and pallor when George is injured (book 7), his direct confrontation with Sirius about the Order's secrecy (book 5), his defense of giving Dudley the toffee on moral grounds, his awareness of the family's financial situation, his private conversations with George about the Bagman blackmail, his role in Potterwatch as a serious resistance broadcaster, and the moment he falls silent watching Percy's estrangement. His death mid-laugh is the ultimate expression of his character — defiant joy to the end.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve the most visible nuances: the George ear-wound scene (Film Scene 22) shows genuine emotional depth, the Hogwarts departure is principled and courageous, the Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes shows entrepreneurial seriousness. The 'Nice night for it!' line in the Battle of Hogwarts captures his defiant joy.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The Sirius confrontation is absent — this is the clearest moment in the books where Fred's humor drops entirely and he speaks with raw emotional directness about his father's safety. The Potterwatch role is absent, removing Fred as a serious resistance voice. The Bagman subplot is absent. The private Fred-George conversations that show them as individuals rather than a unit are absent. The sustained Percy estrangement thread, which Fred navigates with a mixture of contempt and genuine hurt, is compressed. The financial motivation behind the joke shop — which gives Fred's entrepreneurialism a dimension of necessity, not just ambition — is underplayed.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The films preserve the surface of Fred's complexity but lose several of the moments that reveal the depth beneath the humor. The Sirius confrontation and Potterwatch role are the most significant losses — they show Fred as a moral agent and serious resistance fighter, not just a prankster. The result is a Fred who is recognizable but somewhat flattened toward the comic-relief end of his book characterization.

lost_or_transferred_material: Fred's direct confrontation with Sirius at Grimmauld Place ('Easy for you to say, stuck here! I don't see you risking your neck!') — showing moral agency and emotional directness
Fred's role as 'Rapier' on Potterwatch — his transition to serious resistance broadcaster
The Bagman blackmail subplot — Fred and George's persistent moral indignation and attempt to recover their World Cup winnings
The sustained Fred-Percy estrangement thread across books 5-7, including Fred's contempt and underlying hurt
The financial motivation behind Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes — the family poverty dimension
Mrs. Weasley's explicit disapproval of the twins' career choice and the resulting family conflict
Fred's defense of the Ton-Tongue Toffee on moral grounds ('We gave it to him because he's a great bullying git')
The private Fred-George conversations showing them as individuals with separate concerns (Bagman letters, individual reactions to crises)
Fred's extended silence and emotional weight during the long night waiting for news of Arthur Weasley
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function, decision, or act of agency is transferred or removed): Potterwatch role and Sirius confrontation are absent — these are meaningful agency and narrative function losses. Score kept at 72, below 75.
comparative.key_observations: Fred Weasley is one of the more faithfully adapted supporting characters in the film series. His core personality — irreverent, bold, entrepreneurially driven, warm beneath the jokes — is preserved with strong fidelity, and his most iconic moments (Marauder's Map handover, Aging Potion attempt, Hogwarts departure, George's ear-wound exchange, Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes) are all present and well-executed. The primary losses are the moments that reveal depth beneath the humor: his direct confrontation with Sirius, his Potterwatch role as a serious resistance broadcaster, and the sustained Percy estrangement thread. The films also lean more heavily into Fred-and-George as an interchangeable unit, losing some of the individual texture the books give Fred. The result is a portrayal that is recognizably and largely faithfully Fred, but somewhat flattened toward his comic-relief function at the expense of his moral agency and emotional complexity.
